era_context: The Peloponnesian War continues in its fourth year (431–404 BCE). Athens and Sparta remain locked in a grinding conflict with no clear end in sight. The Athenian plague has recently ended (426 BCE), and the city is recovering demographically and militarily. Pericles died in 429 BCE, and new leaders have emerged. The war has disrupted agriculture, trade, and normal civic life across Greece. The Persian Empire remains a major power in the eastern Mediterranean. In Italy, Rome continues to consolidate power in Latium.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are not well integrated into Western historical synthesis)

events.title: Athenian siege and conquest of Mytilene (Lesbos)
events.description: The city of Mytilene on the island of Lesbos, an Athenian ally, revolts against Athenian rule in 428 BCE. Athens responds with a siege that lasts approximately one year (428–427 BCE). The siege is brutal and represents a turning point in Athenian treatment of rebellious allies. After the city's capture, Athens debates the fate of the Mytileneans, ultimately deciding on a harsh punishment that includes the execution of many citizens and the enslavement of others. This event marks a shift toward more ruthless Athenian imperialism.
key_figures: Athenian generals conducting the siege (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides provides a detailed account of the Mytilene revolt and siege, including the famous debate in the Athenian assembly about the city's fate. The dating is well-established.
cross_references: 427 BCE: Siege of Mytilene continues and concludes

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean
events.description: Athens continues aggressive naval campaigns to maintain control of the Aegean and secure grain supplies from the Black Sea region. Naval operations are essential to Athenian survival and the maintenance of its maritime empire. The Mytilene siege is part of a broader campaign to suppress revolts among Athenian allies.
key_figures: Athenian naval commanders (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents ongoing naval operations; specific engagements in 428 BCE are not always individually dated.

events.title: Spartan and allied operations in mainland Greece
events.description: Sparta and its allies continue campaigns to weaken Athenian power and control in central Greece. Boeotian forces and Corinthian contingents remain active in support of Spartan strategy. The war remains a grinding stalemate with neither side able to achieve decisive victory.
key_figures: Spartan ephors and generals (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents the general pattern of Spartan operations; specific events in 428 BCE are not always individually dated.

events.title: Athenian military operations in Sicily
events.description: Athens continues military operations in Sicily, supporting Leontini and other allies against Syracuse. These operations are part of a broader Athenian strategy to expand power in the western Mediterranean and weaken Corinthian influence in the region.
key_figures: Athenian naval commanders (names not consistently recorded)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book III

events.certainty_note: Thucydides documents Athenian operations in Sicily during this period; specific engagements in 428 BCE are not always individually dated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Mytilene revolt is sometimes placed in 427 BCE rather than 428 BCE, with the siege lasting from 428–427 BCE or entirely within 427 BCE. Thucydides' account is detailed, but modern scholars debate the exact chronology. The famous debate in the Athenian assembly about Mytilene's fate (featuring Cleon and Diodotus) is recorded by Thucydides but may be a literary reconstruction rather than a verbatim account. The severity of Athenian punishment of Mytilene is debated; some scholars argue Thucydides exaggerates the harshness of the initial decision.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 428 BCE is heavily dependent on Thucydides, who provides a detailed account of the Mytilene revolt and siege. His narrative is generally reliable but reflects Athenian perspectives and may underrepresent Mytilenean or Spartan viewpoints. The famous debate about Mytilene's fate is a literary highlight of Thucydides' work but may not be a precise record of what was actually said. Archaeological evidence (inscriptions, coins, pottery) provides supplementary information but rarely allows precise annual dating. The record is biased toward military and political events involving major Greek city-states; events in peripheral regions and among non-elite populations are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Mytilene revolt and siege in 428 BCE demonstrates Athenian willingness to use force against allies, shaping future imperial policy
Naval operations in the Aegean include the siege of Mytilene
Athenian and Spartan military operations occur simultaneously as part of the ongoing Peloponnesian War
